message.content:
The fog on Blackwood Point didn’t just obscure vision; it swallowed sound. For Elias Thorne, the keeper of the lighthouse, the silence was a companion as constant as the rhythmic *thrum-click* of the great Fresnel lens above. He had spent thirty years watching the sea chew at the cliffs, his life reduced to the maintenance of glass, oil, and solitude.

It was a Tuesday, the kind of gray, heavy day where the horizon seemed to press against the windows, when he found it.

He was walking the perimeter of the rocky shore, checking for debris that might damage the intake valves of the generator. Half-buried in the wet sand, glinting with an unnatural amber hue, was a bottle. It wasn’t the usual green glass of discarded soda or beer. It was thick, hand-blown, sealed with wax that smelled faintly of lavender and old paper.

Elias picked it up. It was heavy. He shook it; there was no sloshing liquid, only the dry rattle of paper.

Back in the keeper’s quarters, with the storm brewing outside and the lamp humming its steady song, he broke the seal. The wax cracked with a satisfying snap. He pulled out a single sheet of parchment, folded neatly into a square.

The handwriting was elegant, looping, and unmistakably familiar. It was his own.

Elias frowned. He had written many letters in his youth, before the accident that took his wife, Clara, and before he retreated to this rock. But he had never written a letter to himself. He unfolded the paper.

*Dear Elias,*

*If you are reading this, you have finally stopped looking for a reason to stay and started looking for a reason to leave. You think you are guarding the light, but you are only guarding the memory of her. The light doesn
